Hi,  I just want to clarify things here, are you using a computer along with its camera to make a video call but using your phone's Wi-Fi data? (based from what you've mentioned: "I checked that my computer speed was fast enough"). If so, when you do a phone call, that will interfere the connection that Skype is getting from your device and the call will be interrupted. If my understanding above is incorrect and if you're using your phone and its camera to make a video call - same thing will happen. The bottom line is, you're using the data of your phone which I believe is coming from its network (signal). Wi-Fi data is connected through it thus; network disruption will occur. title: My went away
original_question: I lost bing on windows 8 I can not fine on search  I can not find in c drive windows or my programs I can not find a way redownload a new bing win 8 help . it is at app store on laptop
simulator_context:
[Further details provided by the user]

---

[Clarifying questions asked by the support agent]
It sounds like you're referring to the Bing app that appears by default on the Windows 8 Start screen, correct? It's possible that the app was just unpinned from your Start screen. The first thing you might try is to right-click on the Start screen or swipe up from the bottom of the screen and click the All Apps view. Here you should see a comprehensive list of all apps installed on your device. If you find Bing here, then you can just tap+hold to pin it to the Start screen again.  It's also possible that the app was accidentally uninstalled. Bing is a Windows Store (Metro) app,  not a conventional Windows program, so it cannot be managed through the Control Panel. Instead, you should check the Windows Store. Just open the Store app, search for Bing, and it will tell you whether or not it's already installed on your device. If not, you should be able to reinstall it from there. It's a free app, so that should take care of it.  If you're using the Windows 8.1 Preview (or the RTM), be advised that the Bing app no longer exists in this release. The Bing app will not appear in the Windows 8.1 Store. Instead, Bing has been integrated directly into the Search hero, which is accessible from the Search charm on the Charms bar. When you swipe from the right side of the screen (or press Win key + C), tap Search, and enter a search term while searching everywhere, your results will automatically incorporate results from Bing. There's no longer a need for a separate app.  Of course if you prefer a classic search experience, you can still pin the Bing web page to the Start Screen. Just open IE, go to www.bing.com, tap Favorites, and then tap Pin to Start. You'll get a big yellow live tile that just launches the site, shows trending topics, and basically functions like the aforementioned app.  And other tools, such as the Bing Desktop, can still be installed on the Windows 8.1 desktop.  Please let us know if that solves your issue. Thanks!

[Clarifying questions asked by the support agent]
This is controlled by Sections. If you put a Section Break at the end of the first page, you can then change the header with the second section. I have a similar issue; I didn't delete the whole temp folder but did delete everything inside it, and now the start menu will not open. accepted_answer: Some files can be deleted from the Temp folder, but not the folder itself because Windows uses it to hold temporary files in.  It's a bit like your car and it's gas tank. You can drain it, but you still have a fully functional automobile. But if you remove the gas tank, then you're not going to be going anywhere until you replace it.  You now find yourself in that situation with your computer. Until you replace the Temp folder and its contents, your machine is not going to function properly.  I think you're looking at having to Refresh the system in order to get it going again. What you'll have to do first is to create a Recovery disk and copy the manufacturer's partition to that. This means you'll have to go buy an external hard drive with at least 60GB free. But bearing in mind that the cost of a 60GB hard disk and one with a 500GB capacity is barely a matter of cents, you'll be better off with the large capacity drive which you can use for other purposes later. accepted_answer:

It's probably the  "formatting mark" that means that the paragraph has been formatted with one of the following properties:   Keep with next   Keep lines together   Page break before   Suppress line numbers This particular mark is only displayed if you have checked either the Paragraph Marks option or the Show All option in File->Options->Display->Always show these formatting marks on the screen. accepted_answer: I think Aaron means, in the cells in the column with numbers, add three conditional formatting rules to each cell. You can create the rules in one cell and then copy...paste special...formats to the other cells in the column.   One rule checks if the cell's value is equal to the cell that contains the MIN of the column and applies the color; the second rule checks if the cell's value is equal to the cell that contains the MAX of the column and applies that color; the third does the same for the average. [Clarifying questions asked by the support agent]
I'm not an AI user but in the past I've seen the problem with curves and such getting pixelated (actually, they're being rendered as a series of short lines, not getting pixelated, but the effect is similar).  The fix there seems to be to make the shapes as big as possible in AI before exporting to EMF.  Or at least this is how it used to work; apparently AI breaks curves into XX segments per point or inch or whatever.  The bigger you make the graphic, the more segments you get, so the smoother the line.   As far as gradients, my experience has been that you're better off leaving things flat-colored in AI then ungrouping and applying gradients in PPT.  Otherwise, the gradient is liable to turn into a skillion individual shapes, making editing a nightmare.  As to the white borders, not sure, but quite often (I'd say "always, in my experience") EMFs include a background object.  Ungroup the EMF after inserting it into PPT, select the b/g object, delete it, then regroup the remaining shapes.  I can't really say what might help to make the PDF editable; it'd depend on what tools are used and what type of editing is needed.  The above suggestions will probably help, at least somewhat.  But can't AI save directly to PDF?  Why go through Word/PPT at all?
accepted_answer:
Any software that produces EMF will likely have the same kinds of problems.  EMF/WMF graphics are fairly primitive and don't include the ability to handle things like gradient fills, soft edges/shadows and the like.  Instead, programs that export EMF have to perform workarounds; to "preserve" a gradient, they'll export gazillions of rectangles or polygons that, together, look like a single gradient filled shape.  Editing these?  A nightmare!  I would suggest this workflow:  Draw your graphics in any program that works well for you, but stick with simple shapes and lines.  Don't do any gradient fills or other fancy effects.  Export as EMF, import the EMF into PowerPoint, ungroup it and THEN apply any effects you like to the shapes.  Once you do this, the graphic will be in native MS Office format; you can copy/paste it from PPT to Word and it should retain all of its characteristics.  You might, in fact, be able to import the EMF directly into Word, ungroup and apply effects there.  Since PPT is the tool I use most often, I'd rather work there and then copy to Word, but if going directly to Word works well for you, leave PPT out of the equation entirely.

PPT doesn't have a clipping function in quite the same sense that, say, Corel Draw and probably Adobe Illustrator do.  You can't define a clipping path, use it to clip another shape or shapes, and later adjust the clipping path to hide or reveal more of the clipped graphics.  But you can use the Merge Shapes command on the Format | Insert Shapes area to do a one-time clip (ie, you can use intersect to combine an image, for example with a shape and end up with the image clipped to the shape boundaries ... as a new shape that replaces the original shapes).

You want one cell to both sum and count? It's possible, but not recommended as it could be confusing for anyone reading it.  You can just combine the two functions into one formula:  =SUM(A2:A5) & COUNT(B2:B5)  Or to make it easier to read:  ="SUM: " & SUM(A2:A5) & " COUNT: " & COUNT(B2:B5)
